chunk_summary: Key B-complex deficiencies in children, including thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, and pyridoxine, present with distinct syndromes in malnourished or at-risk children. Prompt diagnosis and treatment with specific supplementation and nutritional rehabilitation are critical.
learning_objectives: Identify clinical syndromes associated with thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, and pyridoxine deficiencies in children.
Understand the treatment protocols for B-complex deficiencies, including specific dosages and routes of administration.
Recognize risk factors for B-complex deficiencies, such as severe acute malnutrition (SAM) and refeeding syndrome.
Develop a structured nutritional rehabilitation plan for children with B-complex deficiencies.
key_facts: Thiamine (B1) deficiency can cause encephalopathy, ophthalmoplegia, and wet beriberi; treat with 100 mg IV/IM daily for 3–5 days, followed by 10–20 mg/day orally.
Riboflavin (B2) deficiency presents with angular stomatitis, cheilosis, and glossitis; treat with 5–10 mg/day orally.
Niacin (B3) deficiency (pellagra) causes dermatitis, diarrhea, and neuropsychiatric changes; treat with nicotinamide 100–300 mg/day in divided doses.
Pyridoxine (B6) deficiency can lead to irritability, neuropathy, and seizures; urgent treatment is required during isoniazid therapy or in pyridoxine-dependent epilepsy.
B-complex deficiencies often coexist with iron, folate, and B12 deficiencies, necessitating comprehensive nutritional rehabilitation.
reference_quote: Thiamine deficiency and its prevention and control in major emergencies — https://www.who.int/publications/i/item/WHO-NHD-99.13
